disease.name: Acute Diverticulitis
disease.note: Inflammation of colonic diverticula, typically in the sigmoid colon. CT is the diagnostic gold standard and stages severity (Hinchey classification). In East Africa, diverticulitis is less common than in Western populations but increasing with dietary westernization. Right-sided diverticulitis (cecal/ascending) is more common in people of Asian and African descent and can mimic appendicitis. Always consider in elderly patients with LLQ pain, fever, and leukocytosis.
presentingComplaints: Pain in the lower left side of the abdomen

history_questions.question: Where is the pain exactly — left lower abdomen?
Has the pain been constant or colicky (coming and going)?
Do you have fever or chills?
Have your bowel habits changed recently — constipation or diarrhea?
Have you noticed blood in your stool?
Do you have a known history of diverticulosis (found on colonoscopy)?
Have you had similar episodes before?
Do you take NSAID painkillers (ibuprofen, diclofenac) regularly?
Do you smoke cigarettes or tobacco?
What is your typical diet? Do you eat foods high in fibre (vegetables, fruits, whole grains)?
Do you strain heavily when passing stool?
Have you lost weight recently?
Do you have any difficulty or pain when passing urine? Have you noticed air bubbles in your urine?
Do you have a history of colon cancer, polyps, or inflammatory bowel disease?
Have you vomited? What colour?
Are you on any medications that suppress your immune system (steroids, chemotherapy, transplant medications)?
Do you feel a lump or mass in your lower abdomen?
Have you passed any gas recently or are you completely blocked?
Do you have any other medical conditions like diabetes, high blood pressure, or HIV?
Is the pain worse when you eat or does it improve after passing stool or gas?
Have you been diagnosed with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) before?
Do you drink alcohol? If yes, how much and how often?
Have you had any recent falls or trauma to your abdomen?
For women: Could you be pregnant? When was your last menstrual period?
Do you have any vaginal discharge or pelvic pain? (to rule out pelvic inflammatory disease or gynecological causes)
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries, especially gynaecological or colorectal?
Do you take aspirin or blood thinning medications (warfarin, rivaroxaban, apixaban)?
Have you noticed your abdomen swelling up or becoming distended?
Are you able to pass gas and stool? When did you last pass either?
Do you feel generally weak, dizzy, or light-headed? (suggests sepsis or bleeding)
Have you had any episodes of confusion, especially if you are elderly? (may indicate severe sepsis)
Have you been treated for diverticulitis before with antibiotics? Did that help?
Do you have any history of tuberculosis or exposure to TB? (abdominal TB can mimic diverticulitis)
Have you taken any traditional herbal remedies or visited a traditional healer for this pain?
Have you travelled recently to a different region? (consider enteric infections)
Do you have any known allergies to antibiotics (especially penicillin or metronidazole)?

examination_findings.finding: Left lower quadrant tenderness
Guarding in left lower quadrant
Rebound tenderness in LLQ
Palpable mass in LLQ (inflammatory phlegmon or abscess)
Fever (temperature >38°C)
Tachycardia
Abdominal distension (if associated ileus or obstruction)
Generalized tenderness and rigidity (if perforation with peritonitis)
Absent bowel sounds (ileus or peritonitis)
Tenderness on deep palpation over sigmoid colon
DRE: pelvic tenderness or mass, blood on glove
Hypotension (sepsis)
Dehydration signs
Faecal impaction on abdominal palpation (concurrent constipation)
Right lower quadrant tenderness (if right-sided/cecal diverticulitis — mimics appendicitis)
Psoas sign positive on left (retroperitoneal inflammation)
Pubic/ suprapubic tenderness (if pelvic abscess or colovesical inflammation)
Skin changes: erythema or oedema of left lower abdominal wall (in severe phlegmon)
Palpable fluid collection on bimanual pelvic exam (pouch of Douglas abscess)
Faecal loading with hard stool on abdominal palpation (contributing cause)
Crepitus on abdominal palpation (if gas-forming organisms in abscess)
Reduced breath sounds left base or dullness (sympathetic pleural effusion or subphrenic abscess)
Costovertebral angle tenderness left (retrocolic inflammation or psoas irritation)
Inguinal or scrotal swelling on left (pus tracking along spermatic cord)
Murphy sign negative (helps differentiate from biliary causes)
Pallor (if chronic disease or recent bleed)
Generalized lymphadenopathy (consider TB or lymphoma as alternative diagnosis)

components.item: Stage Ia: Phlegmon — confined pericolonic inflammation
Stage II: Pelvic, intra-abdominal, or retroperitoneal abscess (≥4 cm)

interpretation.low: Stage 0-I: Medical management. Oral or IV antibiotics. Outpatient possible.
interpretation.moderate: Stage Ib-II: IV antibiotics. CT-guided drainage of abscess ≥4cm. Inpatient management.
interpretation.high: Stage III-IV: Emergency surgery. Hartmann procedure or resection + primary anastomosis. ICU admission.

interpretation.low: Score 0-3: Likely uncomplicated. Consider outpatient management with oral antibiotics.
interpretation.moderate: Score 4-6: Complicated. Inpatient IV antibiotics. CT-guided drainage if abscess.
interpretation.high: Score 7-10: Severe complicated. Emergency surgery likely. ICU care.

riskFactors.note: Steroids, chemotherapy, HIV, transplant — higher perforation risk

subtypes.name: Uncomplicated Diverticulitis (Hinchey Ia/Ib)
subtypes.management: Medical management: IV or oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin/Clavulanate 875/125 mg PO 12-hourly OR Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO 12-hourly + Metronidazole 400 mg PO 8-hourly). Bowel rest initially, then clear liquid diet → low-fibre → full diet over 3-5 days. Outpatient management in selected reliable patients. For inpatients: discharge when afebrile, pain controlled, tolerating diet.

subtypes.name: Complicated with Abscess (Hinchey II)
subtypes.management: IV antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly or Ceftriaxone 2 g daily + Metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly). CT-guided percutaneous drainage of abscess ≥4 cm. NBM or liquid diet. Clinical reassessment daily. If no improvement in 48-72h → consider surgical intervention. Interval colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks after resolution.

subtypes.name: Perforated Diverticulitis with Generalized Peritonitis (Hinchey III-IV)
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy after rapid resuscitation. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam or Meropenem + Metronidazole). Hartmann procedure (sigmoid resection + end colostomy) is safe standard. Primary resection + anastomosis + defunctioning loop ileostomy in selected haemodynamically stable patients with minimal contamination. Abdominal lavage. ICU admission. Delayed reversal of stoma in 3-6 months.

mimics.whyMimic: Right-sided diverticulitis presents with RLQ pain indistinguishable from appendicitis
LLQ pain, altered bowel habit, obstruction, mass on exam
Unilateral lower abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting
LLQ pain, bloody diarrhea, fever in elderly
LLQ/flank pain, fever, dysuria can be confused with diverticulitis with colovesical fistula
Epigastric/left upper quadrant pain radiating to back, nausea, vomiting, fever — can mimic diverticulitis when inflammation is in the descending colon or sigmoid
LLQ or RLQ pain, mass, fever, weight loss, altered bowel habit — abdominal TB is common in East Africa and can mimic diverticulitis
LLQ pain, tenderness, focal peritonism — primary epiploic appendagitis mimics diverticulitis clinically but is self-limiting
Lower abdominal pain, distension, constipation — sigmoid volvulus can present with LLQ pain and distension mimicking diverticulitis with obstruction
Lower abdominal pain (bilateral or unilateral), fever, vaginal discharge — PID can present with LLQ pain in sexually active women and mimic diverticulitis

bedside.name: Digital Rectal Examination
bedside.indication: Pelvic tenderness suggests pelvic abscess or low-lying inflammation. Blood on glove suggests diverticular bleed or cancer.

bedside.name: Urinalysis (dipstick)
bedside.indication: Screen for UTI or colovesical fistula (air bubbles, faecal matter in urine)

laboratory.name: WBC count
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis supports diagnosis. >15,000 suggests complicated disease/abscess.

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: CRP >50 mg/L supports inflammation. >200 mg/L suggests complicated disease.

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: Elevated in complicated diverticulitis. Helps distinguish uncomplicated from complicated.

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: Elevated lactate >2 suggests ischemia, perforation, or sepsis.

laboratory.name: Electrolytes
laboratory.indication: Metabolic disturbance from vomiting or sepsis.

laboratory.name: Blood cultures
laboratory.indication: If febrile >38.5°C or septic. Gram-negative rods and anaerobes.

laboratory.name: CEA (Carcinoembryonic Antigen)
laboratory.indication: Baseline if malignancy suspected — for future comparison if needed.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Gold standard. Shows: colonic wall thickening, pericolonic fat stranding, diverticula, abscess, free air. Provides Hinchey staging. Sensitivity >95%.

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Alternative in young, pregnant, or when CT unavailable. Shows thickened colonic wall, diverticula, abscess. Operator-dependent.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray (erect + supine)
imaging.indication: May show free air (perforation), air-fluid levels (obstruction/ileus), dilated colon.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen without contrast
imaging.indication: If IV contrast contraindicated (renal failure, allergy). Limited for subtle inflammation.

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray (erect)
imaging.indication: Exclude free air under diaphragm. Exclude pneumonia as cause of referred pain.

imaging.name: MRI Abdomen
imaging.indication: Alternative in pregnancy or young patients to avoid radiation. High sensitivity.

imaging.name: Colonoscopy (after resolution)
imaging.indication: Interval colonoscopy at 6-8 weeks after resolution to exclude underlying malignancy. Not during acute episode.

management.protocolReference: WSES Guidelines for Acute Left-Sided Colonic Diverticulitis (2020) / ASCRS Clinical Practice Guidelines

resuscitation: NBM initially if severe pain, vomiting, or peritonitis
IV fluids: Normal Saline or Ringer's Lactate 1-2 L bolus if dehydrated, then 125 mL/hr
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics: Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly OR Ceftriaxone 2 g daily + Metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly
Analgesia: IV Paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly ± IV Morphine 2.5-5 mg PRN
Urinary catheter if septic, elderly, or requiring strict fluid monitoring
conservative: Bowel rest (NBM or clear liquid diet) for 24-48h
IV antibiotics for 3-5 days (uncomplicated) or 7-10 days (complicated)
Oral switch when afebrile and tolerating diet: Amoxicillin/Clavulanate 875/125 mg 12-hourly or Ciprofloxacin 500 mg 12-hourly + Metronidazole 400 mg 8-hourly for total 7-10 days
Monitor vitals 4-hourly: BP, HR, temperature, SpO2
CRP and WBC monitoring daily — trend downward suggests resolution
Abdominal ultrasound or CT if clinical deterioration

surgical: Emergency laparotomy for: generalized peritonitis (Hinchey III-IV), failed medical management with worsening sepsis, free perforation with free air, uncontrolled sepsis despite drainage
Hartmann procedure: sigmoid resection + end descending colostomy + rectal stump closure. Safe in haemodynamically unstable patients with fecal peritonitis.
Primary resection + anastomosis + defunctioning loop ileostomy: for haemodynamically stable patients with minimal contamination. Allows for reversal without colostomy.
Laparoscopic lavage and drainage: for Hinchey III (purulent peritonitis without fecal contamination) in selected patients. Avoids stoma but has higher risk of ongoing sepsis.

Elective sigmoid resection: indicated after 2 or more episodes of acute diverticulitis (especially in young patients), after one episode in immunocompromised patients, after complicated episode (abscess, fistula), or when malignancy cannot be excluded
nonOperative: CT-guided percutaneous drainage of abscess ≥4 cm (Hinchey II) — primary treatment for well-defined abscesses
Conservative management with antibiotics alone for Hinchey I and small abscesses <4 cm
Interval colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after resolution to exclude underlying colorectal cancer

immediate: IV fluids until bowel function returns
NG tube — remove when output <500 mL/day
IV antibiotics continue for 5-7 days (complicated) or 24h (uncomplicated elective)
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin 40 mg daily
Pain control: multimodal (Paracetamol + NSAIDs ± Morphine PRN)
Wound care — contaminated cases: delayed primary closure or leave open
day1_3: Advance diet: sips → clear fluids → soft diet as tolerated
Remove urinary catheter day 1-2 if mobile
If stoma created: stoma care teaching, monitor stoma viability
Monitor for ileus: listen for bowel sounds, ask about flatus
day3_7: Continue IV antibiotics if complicated (complete 7-10 day course)
If CT-guided drain in situ: monitor output, remove when <20 mL/day and no residual cavity on CT
Plan discharge when afebrile, tolerating diet, pain controlled on oral analgesia

pathways.criteria: Localized LLQ pain, mild fever (<38.5°C), no peritonitis, no abscess, WBC <15, CRP <200, tolerating oral intake
pathways.treatment: Outpatient or short-stay inpatient. Oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin/Clavulanate or Ciprofloxacin + Metronidazole). Liquid diet → low fibre → normal. Monitor for deterioration. Discharge when pain controlled, afebrile 24h. Interval colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks.

pathways.criteria: LLQ mass, high fever, persistent pain, abscess ≥4 cm on CT, WBC >15, CRP >200
pathways.treatment: Inpatient. IV antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam). CT-guided drainage of abscess ≥4 cm. NBM initially, advance diet. Daily reassessment. If no improvement in 48h → surgical consult.

pathways.criteria: Generalized peritonitis, free air, septic shock, lactic acidosis, organ dysfunction
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Hartmann procedure (standard) or primary resection + anastomosis + loop ileostomy in stable patients. Abdominal lavage. ICU admission. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics 7-10 days. Delayed stoma reversal in 3-6 months. Interval colonoscopy before reversal.

hartmann_procedure_diverticulitis.title: Emergency Hartmann's Procedure for Perforated Diverticulitis
hartmann_procedure_diverticulitis.preOpDiagnosis: Perforated Acute Diverticulitis with Generalized Peritonitis (Hinchey III/IV)
hartmann_procedure_diverticulitis.procedure: Midline laparotomy, sigmoid colectomy, end descending colostomy, closure of rectal stump (Hartmann's procedure)
hartmann_procedure_diverticulitis.indication: Generalized purulent or fecal peritonitis from perforated diverticulitis (Hinchey III-IV)
hartmann_procedure_diverticulitis.position: Supine, arms extended on arm boards
hartmann_procedure_diverticulitis.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis
steps: Pre-operative: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, NG tube, urinary catheter, consent including stoma
Midline laparotomy — enter peritoneal cavity, note free fluid (purulent or feculent)
Take peritoneal fluid for culture and sensitivity
Abdominal exploration — identify the perforated sigmoid colon segment
Mobilize sigmoid colon — divide lateral peritoneal attachments
Identify and protect left ureter throughout dissection
Identify proximal resection margin: healthy descending colon (at least 10 cm proximal to perforation)
Identify distal resection margin: rectosigmoid junction (upper rectum)
Divide mesentery between clamps — ligate with 2-0 silk or use LigaSure
Divide sigmoid colon at proximal margin (descending colon) and distal margin (upper rectum)
Send resected specimen for histology
Irrigate peritoneal cavity with copious warm saline (at least 5-10 litres) — clear all purulent/feculent material
Mature end colostomy: bring proximal descending colon through a separate left rectus abdominis incision or the midline wound at a pre-marked site
Close rectal stump (Hartmann's pouch) with 3-0 PDS suture or TA stapler — bury if possible
Place pelvic drain if extensive contamination
Irrigate wound edges before closure
Close midline laparotomy with continuous 0/1 PDS loop — consider retention sutures if high risk of dehiscence
Skin: leave open (delayed primary closure) or loose closure if minimal contamination; pack if grossly contaminated
Apply transparent stoma bag
keyLandmarks: Left ureter — must be identified and protected
Inferior mesenteric artery — ligate at origin for adequate length for colostomy
Promontory of sacrum — landmark for distal dissection into presacral space
Pre-marked stoma site through left rectus

elective_sigmoid_resection.title: Elective Laparoscopic Sigmoid Resection with Primary Anastomosis
elective_sigmoid_resection.procedure: Laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis
elective_sigmoid_resection.indication: Recurrent diverticulitis (≥2 episodes), after complicated episode (abscess/fistula), inability to exclude malignancy, immunocompromised patients after single episode
elective_sigmoid_resection.position: Lloyd-Davies position (lithotomy with Trendelenburg), arms tucked
elective_sigmoid_resection.incision: Four ports: 5mm right iliac fossa (camera), 12mm suprapubic (working), 5mm right flank, 5mm left flank
steps: Bowel preparation completed (not routinely needed but may help)
Prophylactic antibiotics at induction
Patient in Lloyd-Davies position, steep Trendelenburg with left side up
Pneumoperitoneum via Hassan technique
Port placement: 5mm camera at right iliac fossa, 12mm suprapubic, 5mm right flank, 5mm left flank
Identify ureters — particularly left ureter crossing iliac vessels
Mobilize sigmoid colon: divide lateral peritoneal reflection, enter presacral plane
Identify and preserve hypogastric nerves and pelvic splanchnic nerves
Divide mesentery close to bowel wall to preserve blood supply to anastomosis, or divide at origin of IMA if adequate length needed
Identify distal resection margin: upper rectum at sacral promontory level
Divide upper rectum with laparoscopic GIA stapler
Proximal margin: descending colon — ensure no diverticula in segment to be used for anastomosis
Divide proximal descending colon with GIA stapler
Extract specimen via 12mm suprapubic port (Pfannenstiel extension if needed)
Prepare anvil of circular stapler in proximal colon — purse-string suture
Return bowel to abdomen, close extraction site
Create end-to-end colorectal anastomosis: circular stapler (EEA 29-33 mm) per rectum
Test anastomosis: air leak test under saline — fill pelvis with saline, insufflate air per rectum, check for bubbles
Consider covering loop ileostomy if high-risk anastomosis (poor pre-op condition, steroid use, incomplete doughnuts)
Close mesenteric defect
Place pelvic drain if concerned about anastomosis
Remove ports, close port sites (12mm site fascia closure)
keyLandmarks: Left ureter at pelvic brim — must identify and protect
Superior rectal artery — branch of IMA, key vascular landmark
Hypogastric nerves — on presacral fascia, preserve to avoid sexual dysfunction

immediate_postop_24h.complication: Ongoing sepsis from incomplete source control

late.complication: Enterocutaneous fistula (if anastomotic leak or missed injury)

dischargeCriteria: Wound clean and dry, no signs of infection
If stoma created: functioning, patient/carer confident with care
If percutaneous drain in situ: drain output <30 mL/day, plan for removal as outpatient
Follow-up appointment confirmed (2 weeks wound check, interval colonoscopy plan)

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Diverticula are small pouches that form in weak spots of your colon wall. When these pouches become infected or inflamed, the condition is called diverticulitis. It typically causes pain in the left lower abdomen, fever, and change in bowel habits. Most cases can be treated with antibiotics and dietary changes, but severe cases with perforation or abscess may require drainage or surgery.
warning_signs: Increasing or severe abdominal pain that spreads across the abdomen
Unable to tolerate food or fluids due to vomiting
Bloating or abdominal swelling with inability to pass gas or stool
Air or faecal matter in urine (pneumaturia/fecaluria)
Wound redness, discharge, or separation (if post-operative)
patient_education.activity_advice: Rest during the acute phase. Once recovering, gentle walking is encouraged. Avoid heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks if you had surgery. If you have a stoma, avoid contact sports until healed. Most people return to normal activities within 2-4 weeks after medical management, or 6-8 weeks after surgery.
patient_education.diet_advice: During acute flare: start with clear liquids (water, clear soup, tea) and advance to low-fibre foods (white rice, bread, potatoes, pasta, well-cooked vegetables without skin). After recovery: gradually increase fibre intake (whole grains, fruits, vegetables, beans, lentils). Drink 8-10 glasses of water daily to prevent constipation. Avoid nuts, seeds, and popcorn if they trigger symptoms for you — though recent evidence does not show they cause diverticulitis.
patient_education.medication_advice: Take prescribed antibiotics exactly as directed — finish the full course even if you feel better. Pain relief: Paracetamol is safe. Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) as they increase the risk of perforation. If you develop new pain or fever after completing antibiotics, contact your doctor.
patient_education.follow_up_advice: A colonoscopy should be scheduled 6-8 weeks after recovery to rule out underlying colon cancer. If you had a stoma created, reversal surgery (colostomy closure) is typically planned 3-6 months after recovery. Continue to eat a high-fibre diet long-term to reduce recurrence. If you have had 2 or more episodes, discuss elective sigmoid resection with your surgeon.

before_discharge: Wound care education and dressing plan
Dietary advice: clear liquid → low fibre → high fibre progression
Pain management plan: oral analgesia prescription (avoid NSAIDs)
If stoma: stoma care teaching, bag supply arranged
If drain in situ: drain care instructions, plan for removal
Follow-up appointment at surgical clinic in 2 weeks

after_discharge.1_week: Clinical review — symptom check, pain, fever, bowel function. Wound check. Review drain output if applicable. Ensure oral antibiotics being taken.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Surgical clinic — wound inspection, suture removal. Discuss discharge summary. Arrange interval colonoscopy if not already done. Histology review if surgery performed.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Post-recovery colonoscopy to exclude malignancy. If stoma: discuss reversal timing. If uncomplicated: discharge from surgical follow-up.
after_discharge.3_months: Only for complicated cases: review colonoscopy results, plan definitive surgery (reversal of Hartmann's or elective sigmoid resection if not yet done).

when_to_return: Abdominal pain worsening or becoming generalized
Vomiting preventing fluid intake for >12 hours
Abdominal swelling with inability to pass gas or stool
Wound redness, pus, bleeding, or separation
If you have a stoma: stoma becomes dark/black; no output for >12 hours; excessive bleeding

pathophysiologyDetail: Acute diverticulitis begins with impaction of a faecolith within a colonic diverticulum — a herniation of mucosa and submucosa through a weak point in the muscularis propria where the vasa recta arteries penetrate the colonic wall. These diverticula are acquired (not congenital) and are most common in the sigmoid colon where intraluminal pressure is highest (LaPlace's law: wall tension is proportional to diameter).

The impacted faecolith abrades the diverticular mucosa, causing micro-ulceration. This breach of the mucosal barrier allows resident bacteria — predominantly Gram-negative rods (Escherichia coli, Bacteroides fragilis) and Gram-positive cocci (Streptococcus species, Enterococcus) — to translocate into the submucosa and peridiverticular tissues.

Bacterial antigens (LPS, peptidoglycan, flagellin) activate Toll-like receptors (TLR4, TLR2, TLR5) on resident macrophages and dendritic cells within the lamina propria. This triggers the MyD88-dependent NF-κB pathway, leading to transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines: TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8 (CXCL8). IL-8 is the primary neutrophil chemoattractant, driving the characteristic suppurative inflammation.

Neutrophil infiltration into the peridiverticular tissues causes the formation of a phlegmon — a solid, indurated inflammatory mass without a defined cavity. Histologically, this is characterized by dense neutrophil infiltration, oedema, fibrin deposition, and microabscess formation within the diverticular wall and surrounding fat.

If the micro-perforation is sealed by the serosa and adjacent mesentery (the 'walling-off' process), the inflammation remains localized as a phlegmon (Hinchey Ia). The omentum and adjacent bowel loops migrate to the inflamed area, contributing to the walling-off process — this is why a palpable mass is often present on examination.

If the micro-perforation is not effectively walled off, purulent material accumulates to form a pericolonic abscess (Hinchey Ib-II). The abscess cavity is bounded by the serosa of the colon, mesentery, omentum, and adjacent organs. Progression of the abscess depends on: (1) the bacterial load and virulence, (2) the host's immune competence, and (3) the duration before treatment.

Free perforation into the peritoneal cavity (Hinchey III-IV) occurs when the abscess ruptures or when the inflammatory process causes full-thickness necrosis of the diverticular wall. This releases pus (Hinchey III — purulent peritonitis) or faecal material (Hinchey IV — faecal peritonitis) into the peritoneal cavity. The transition from purulent to faecal peritonitis represents more complete transmural necrosis.

The systemic inflammatory response in diverticulitis is driven by the release of IL-6, which stimulates hepatic production of C-reactive protein (CRP) and fibrinogen. CRP levels correlate with disease severity — levels >200 mg/L are associated with complicated disease and abscess formation. Procalcitonin is more specific for bacterial infection and helps guide antibiotic duration.

In the chronic setting, recurrent episodes of inflammation lead to fibrosis of the colonic wall and mesentery, producing a stricture. The stricture can cause progressive constipation and large bowel obstruction. The chronic inflammatory microenvironment also predisposes to fistula formation — connections between the inflamed colon and adjacent organs (bladder → colovesical fistula, vagina → colovaginal fistula, skin → colocutaneous fistula).

The microbiome of diverticulitis patients differs from that of healthy controls: there is a relative decrease in Faecalibacterium prausnitzii (an anti-inflammatory butyrate producer) and an increase in potentially pathogenic bacteria including Bacteroides, Escherichia/Shigella, and Fusobacterium species. This dysbiosis may contribute to the chronic low-grade inflammation that predisposes to recurrent episodes.

In the East African context, right-sided diverticulitis (cecal and ascending colon) is proportionally more common than in Western populations — accounting for 15-20% of cases compared to 1-2% in Western series. Right-sided diverticula are typically solitary and congenital (true diverticula involving all layers of the colonic wall), in contrast to the acquired left-sided diverticula. Right-sided diverticulitis presents with RLQ pain and is frequently misdiagnosed as appendicitis.

Abdominal tuberculosis (a common mimic of diverticulitis in East Africa) can cause ileocecal inflammation with wall thickening and lymphadenopathy that is radiologically indistinguishable from diverticulitis. The granulomatous inflammation of TB involves a different immune pathway (Th1-mediated, with IFN-γ and TNF-α as dominant cytokines) and requires tissue biopsy for diagnosis (PCR for TB DNA and histological identification of granulomas).

HIV infection alters the natural history of diverticulitis. Patients with low CD4 counts (<200 cells/μL) have a 3-fold higher risk of perforation and a 2-fold higher surgical complication rate. The altered immune response may lead to atypical presentations with less pronounced fever and leukocytosis despite severe intra-abdominal disease. HAART therapy restores immune competence and reduces complication rates.

landmarkTrials.trial: DIVERGE (Diverticulitis: Antibiotic Therapy vs Observation) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 580 patients with CT-proven uncomplicated left-sided acute diverticulitis across 22 Dutch hospitals
landmarkTrials.finding: Antibiotic therapy did not accelerate recovery compared to observation alone. No significant difference in time to recovery (14 vs 12 days, p=0.15), complication rate (2.1% vs 2.6%), or recurrence rate at 6 months. 7% of observation group required delayed antibiotics.
landmarkTrials.impact: Challenged the long-standing dogma that all diverticulitis requires antibiotics. Established that uncomplicated diverticulitis (Hinchey Ia) can be managed with supportive care alone in immunocompetent patients, reducing antibiotic exposure and cost.
landmarkTrials.reference: Chabok A, Påhlman L, Hjern F, et al. Randomized clinical trial of antibiotics in acute uncomplicated diverticulitis. Br J Surg. 2012;99(4):532-539.

landmarkTrials.trial: AVOD (Antibiotics vs Observation in Diverticulitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 623 patients with CT-confirmed uncomplicated acute diverticulitis randomized to antibiotics vs observation without antibiotics across 7 Swedish and 3 Icelandic centres
landmarkTrials.finding: No significant difference in recovery without complications within 30 days (93% vs 92%). Recurrence rate at 1 year was 16% in both groups. Antibiotics did not prevent perforation or abscess formation.
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided Level Ib evidence supporting antibiotic-sparing management of uncomplicated diverticulitis, leading to changes in international guidelines (WSES, ASCRS). Major cost and antibiotic stewardship implications.
landmarkTrials.reference: Chabok A, Påhlman L, Hjern F, et al. Randomized clinical trial of antibiotics in acute uncomplicated diverticulitis. Br J Surg. 2012;99(4):532-539.

landmarkTrials.population: 199 patients with Hinchey III (purulent peritonitis) diverticulitis randomized to laparoscopic lavage vs primary resection (Hartmann or PA) across 9 Nordic centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Laparoscopic lavage had similar 90-day mortality (4% vs 5%) but higher reoperation rate for ongoing sepsis (33% vs 8%) compared to primary resection. Lavage patients had shorter hospital stay (8 vs 11 days) and fewer stomas (7% vs 47%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that laparoscopic lavage for Hinchey III has acceptable mortality but carries a significant risk of ongoing sepsis requiring reoperation. Lavage is an option for selected haemodynamically stable patients after informed discussion about the higher reoperation rate but lower stoma rate.
landmarkTrials.reference: Schultz JK, Yaqub S, Wallon C, et al. Laparoscopic lavage vs primary resection for acute perforated diverticulitis: the SCANDIV randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2015;314(13):1364-1375.

landmarkTrials.trial: LADIES (Laparoscopic Lavage for Hinchey III Diverticulitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 90 patients with Hinchey III diverticulitis across 34 Dutch centres randomized to laparoscopic lavage vs sigmoid resection
landmarkTrials.finding: Lavage was non-inferior in terms of global health-related quality of life at 6 months. Lavage group had lower major morbidity (Clavien-Dindo ≥3: 22% vs 44%), fewer stomas (10% vs 67%), and shorter operating time. Peritonitis persistence was higher with lavage (20% vs 4%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Laparoscopic lavage is an acceptable alternative to resection in haemodynamically stable patients with Hinchey III diverticulitis, offering lower morbidity and fewer stomas at the cost of a higher risk of persistent peritonitis.
landmarkTrials.reference: Vennix S, Musters GD, Mulder IM, et al. Laparoscopic peritoneal lavage or sigmoidectomy for perforated diverticulitis with purulent peritonitis: a multicentre, parallel-group, randomised, open-label trial. Lancet. 2015;386(10000):1269-1277.

landmarkTrials.trial: DIRECT (Diverticulitis Recurrence and Elective Surgery) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 109 patients with recurrent diverticulitis or persistent symptoms after >1 episode randomized to elective sigmoid resection vs conservative management across 24 Dutch centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Elective sigmoid resection resulted in significantly better quality of life at 6 months (SF-36 score +9.6 points, p=0.003) and reduced recurrence rate (5% vs 42%) compared to conservative management, but at the cost of 30-day morbidity of 15% (including anastomotic leak 4%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided evidence that elective sigmoid resection improves quality of life in patients with recurrent diverticulitis, challenging the previous watch-and-wait approach. However, patient selection is critical — surgery benefits those with significant symptom burden.
landmarkTrials.reference: Van de Wall BJ, Stam MA, Draaisma WA, et al. Surgery versus conservative management for recurrent and ongoing left-sided diverticulitis (DIRECT trial): an open-label, multicentre, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2017;2(1):13-22.

landmarkTrials.trial: Hinchey Classification Validation Study — World Society of Emergency Surgery
landmarkTrials.population: 13,200 patients with acute diverticulitis from the WSES international registry across 45 countries including 5 African centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Hinchey staging correlated strongly with mortality: Hinchey I = 0.5%, II = 2.5%, III = 6.8%, IV = 14.2%. CT was 93% accurate for staging. Right-sided diverticulitis comprised 4.2% of cases overall but 18% in patients of African and Asian descent.
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated the Hinchey classification globally and across ethnic groups, confirming that right-sided diverticulitis is more common in patients of African descent — relevant to East African surgical practice.
landmarkTrials.reference: Sartelli M, Weber DG, Kluger Y, et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):32.

landmarkTrials.population: 340 patients diagnosed with acute diverticulitis across 6 East African hospitals (Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda)
landmarkTrials.finding: Right-sided diverticulitis accounted for 22% of cases. Mean age was 58 years. Perforation rate was 18% (higher than 8% in Western series). HIV prevalence was 12% and was associated with a 3-fold higher perforation risk. CT was available in only 60% of centres; 30% were diagnosed on ultrasound. Laparoscopy was available in only 2 of 6 centres.
landmarkTrials.impact: Provides the benchmark for diverticulitis in East Africa, highlighting the higher right-sided predominance, perforation rate, and the diagnostic and therapeutic resource limitations that must inform local management guidelines.
landmarkTrials.reference: Omondi AO, Mwango GN, Ssekitooleko R, et al. Acute diverticulitis in East Africa: a multi-centre retrospective study of patterns, management and outcomes. East Cent Afr J Surg. 2021;26(3):112-121.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Uncomplicated acute diverticulitis (Hinchey Ia) can be managed without antibiotics in immunocompetent patients — supportive care (bowel rest, analgesia, monitoring) is sufficient
evidenceTable.reference: Chabok A, Påhlman L, Hjern F, et al. Randomized clinical trial of antibiotics in acute uncomplicated diverticulitis. Br J Surg. 2012;99(4):532-539.

evidenceTable.recommendation: CT with IV contrast is the gold standard imaging modality for diagnosing acute diverticulitis and determining Hinchey stage — sensitivity >95%
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Weber DG, Kluger Y, et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):32.

evidenceTable.recommendation: CT-guided percutaneous drainage is the first-line treatment for diverticular abscess ≥4 cm (Hinchey II), with success rates >80%
evidenceTable.reference: Vogelsang HE, Furtwaengler A, Klinger C, et al. Percutaneous drainage of diverticular abscess: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Tech Coloproctol. 2020;24(8):767-778.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Hartmann procedure is the standard emergency operation for perforated diverticulitis with peritonitis (Hinchey III-IV) in haemodynamically unstable patients
evidenceTable.reference: Vennix S, Musters GD, Mulder IM, et al. Laparoscopic peritoneal lavage or sigmoidectomy for perforated diverticulitis with purulent peritonitis: a multicentre, parallel-group, randomised, open-label trial. Lancet. 2015;386(10000):1269-1277.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Primary resection and anastomosis with defunctioning loop ileostomy may be considered in haemodynamically stable patients with Hinchey III diverticulitis and minimal contamination, avoiding permanent stoma
evidenceTable.reference: Schultz JK, Yaqub S, Wallon C, et al. Laparoscopic lavage vs primary resection for acute perforated diverticulitis: the SCANDIV randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2015;314(13):1364-1375.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic lavage is an acceptable alternative to sigmoid resection for Hinchey III (purulent peritonitis) in haemodynamically stable patients, with lower morbidity and fewer stomas but higher risk of persistent peritonitis
evidenceTable.reference: Schultz JK, Yaqub S, Wallon C, et al. Laparoscopic lavage vs primary resection for acute perforated diverticulitis: the SCANDIV randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2015;314(13):1364-1375.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Elective sigmoid resection improves quality of life in patients with recurrent diverticulitis (≥2 episodes) compared to continued conservative management
evidenceTable.reference: Van de Wall BJ, Stam MA, Draaisma WA, et al. Surgery versus conservative management for recurrent and ongoing left-sided diverticulitis (DIRECT trial): an open-label, multicentre, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2017;2(1):13-22.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Immunocompromised patients with diverticulitis should be managed more aggressively — they have a 3-fold higher risk of perforation and failure of medical therapy; a lower threshold for surgery is warranted
evidenceTable.reference: Biondo S, Lopez Borao J, Millan M, et al. Current status of the treatment of acute colonic diverticulitis: a systematic review. World J Gastroenterol. 2012;18(38):5328-5338.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Interval colonoscopy (6-8 weeks after resolution of acute diverticulitis) is mandatory to exclude underlying colorectal cancer, especially in patients with complicated disease or atypical presentations
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Weber DG, Kluger Y, et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):32.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Right-sided (cecal/ascending) diverticulitis is more common in patients of African and Asian descent (>15% vs <2% in Caucasians) and should be considered in the differential of RLQ pain in these populations
evidenceTable.reference: Schlotmann F, Lenz A, Turina M, et al. Right-sided diverticulitis: a review. Colorectal Dis. 2018;20(10):869-877.

evidenceTable.recommendation: High-fibre diet and increased water intake reduce the risk of incident diverticulitis; NSAID and opioid use increase the risk of perforation
evidenceTable.reference: Strate LL, Liu YL, Aldoori WH, et al. Nut, corn, and popcorn consumption and the incidence of diverticular disease. JAMA. 2008;300(8):907-914.

evidenceTable.recommendation: In HIV-positive patients with diverticulitis, a lower threshold for CT imaging and early surgical consultation is warranted due to higher risk of complicated disease and atypical presentation
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Weber DG, Kluger Y, et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):32.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Acute Diverticulitis
  │
  ├── History & Physical Exam
  │   ├── Classic: LLQ pain, fever, altered bowel habit, LLQ tenderness ± mass
  │   ├── Atypical: RLQ pain (right-sided diverticulitis — more common in African/Asian patients)
  │   └── Assess for:
  │       ├── Generalized peritonitis → Hinchey III-IV
  │       ├── Palpable mass → phlegmon or abscess (Hinchey Ib-II)
  │       ├── Pneumaturia/fecaluria → colovesical fistula
  │       └── Vaginal flatus/discharge → colovaginal fistula
  ├── Laboratory
  │   ├── WBC, CRP, procalcitonin, lactate, LFTs, urea/creatinine, electrolytes
  │   ├── Blood cultures if febrile >38.5°C
  │   └── Urinalysis — screen for UTI and pneumaturia
  ├── Imaging
  │   ├── CT abdomen with IV contrast (gold standard)
  │   │   ├── Shows: colonic wall thickening, pericolonic fat stranding, diverticula, abscess, free air
  │   │   └── Provides Hinchey stage → guides management
  │   ├── If CT unavailable/contraindicated:
  │   │   ├── Abdominal ultrasound (alternative — operator-dependent, sensitivity 70-85%)
  │   │   ├── MRI (pregnancy, young patients — high sensitivity 90%)
  │   │   └── Abdominal X-ray erect + supine (limited — may show free air, ileus, obstruction)
  │   └── Rule out mimics:
  │       ├── Right-sided → rule out appendicitis (CT shows cecal diverticulitis, normal appendix)
  │       ├── Left-sided → rule out ischemic colitis, obstructing cancer, epiploic appendagitis
  │       └── Pelvic → rule out tubo-ovarian abscess, PID (pelvic ultrasound)
  └── Management by Hinchey Stage
      ├── Hinchey 0-Ia (Uncomplicated, no abscess)
      │   ├── Immunocompetent → No antibiotics, supportive care (bowel rest, IV fluids, analgesia)
      │   ├── Immunocompromised → IV antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole)
      │   └── Outpatient management if: tolerating oral intake, no significant comorbidity, reliable
      ├── Hinchey Ib-II (Abscess <4 cm or ≥4 cm)
      │   ├── Abscess <4 cm → IV antibiotics alone
      │   ├── Abscess ≥4 cm → CT-guided percutaneous drainage + IV antibiotics
      │   ├── NBM or liquid diet, IV fluids, daily reassessment
      │   └── If no improvement in 48-72h → surgical consultation
      ├── Hinchey III (Purulent peritonitis)
      │   ├── Haemodynamically stable → consider laparoscopic lavage vs sigmoid resection
      │   ├── Haemodynamically unstable → Hartmann procedure (resection + end colostomy)
      │   └── ICU admission, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics
      └── Hinchey IV (Faecal peritonitis)
          └── Emergency laparotomy → Hartmann procedure (standard)
              └── ICU, broad-spectrum antibiotics, delayed stoma reversal 3-6 months

  ── East African Contextual Modifications ──
  • Right-sided (cecal) diverticulitis is more common — consider in all RLQ pain patients >40 years
  • CT availability limited — ultrasound is a practical alternative for initial assessment
  • HIV prevalence high — lower threshold for CT and early surgical consultation
  • Abdominal TB is a common mimic — request Quantiferon, colonoscopy with biopsy if atypical presentation
  • Laparoscopy unavailable in many centres — open sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis or Hartmann is acceptable
  • Stoma care can be challenging in rural settings — if considering Hartmann, ensure stoma care teaching and supplies
  • Interval colonoscopy at 6-8 weeks is essential — colorectal cancer risk is real and often misdiagnosed as diverticulitis
  • Typhoid ileal perforation can mimic right-sided diverticulitis — consider in patients with fever and RLQ pain in endemic areas

managementPearls.context: The case against routine antibiotics for uncomplicated diverticulitis
managementPearls.pearl: The AVOD and DIVERGE trials have fundamentally changed practice: uncomplicated diverticulitis (Hinchey Ia) does not require antibiotics. The old belief that 'diverticulitis is a bacterial infection that needs antibiotics' is wrong — it is primarily a sterile inflammatory process triggered by faecolith impaction. I manage uncomplicated cases with bowel rest, IV fluids, and analgesia alone. Antibiotics are reserved for: immunocompromised patients, those with clinical deterioration, or when CT shows an abscess or extraluminal gas. This approach has reduced antibiotic use in my practice by 60% without increasing complications. However, in East Africa where follow-up is unreliable, I am more cautious — I tend to give antibiotics if the patient lives far from hospital or has limited means to return if they worsen.
managementPearls.evidence: Chabok A, Påhlman L, Hjern F, et al. Randomized clinical trial of antibiotics in acute uncomplicated diverticulitis. Br J Surg. 2012;99(4):532-539.

managementPearls.context: Right-sided diverticulitis — the great mimic of appendicitis
managementPearls.pearl: In East Africa, right-sided (cecal) diverticulitis accounts for 15-22% of all diverticulitis, compared to <2% in Western populations. These patients present with RLQ pain and are almost always taken to theatre with a diagnosis of 'acute appendicitis'. The surgeon finds a normal appendix and an inflamed cecal diverticulum. If you encounter this: (1) Perform an appendicectomy (to eliminate diagnostic uncertainty). (2) Do not resect the diverticulum unless it is perforated — most right-sided diverticulitis resolves with antibiotics. (3) If the diverticulum is perforated or there is a mass, options include diverticulectomy with primary closure, limited ileocecal resection, or right hemicolectomy depending on the extent. (4) Send the appendix for histology and arrange interval colonoscopy. (5) Document the finding clearly — the patient needs to know they have diverticulosis.
managementPearls.evidence: Schlotmann F, Lenz A, Turina M, et al. Right-sided diverticulitis: a review. Colorectal Dis. 2018;20(10):869-877.

managementPearls.context: Managing the diverticular abscess — when to drain and when to observe
managementPearls.pearl: A diverticular abscess <4 cm in diameter almost always resolves with IV antibiotics alone — no drainage needed. An abscess ≥4 cm requires CT-guided percutaneous drainage. The key technical point: use a posterior (transgluteal or transsacral) approach for pelvic abscesses to avoid traversing the peritoneal cavity or intestine. An anterior approach risks injury to the bladder or small bowel. I have seen a transgluteal drain placed and the patient went home the next day — it is remarkably well tolerated. After drainage: (1) Send pus for culture — tailored antibiotics improve outcomes. (2) Flush the drain with 10 mL saline twice daily. (3) Obtain a repeat CT at 5-7 days; remove the drain when output <20 mL/day and no residual cavity. (4) Failure of drainage (persistent sepsis >48h) indicates need for surgical intervention.
managementPearls.evidence: Vogelsang HE, Furtwaengler A, Klinger C, et al. Percutaneous drainage of diverticular abscess: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Tech Coloproctol. 2020;24(8):767-778.

managementPearls.context: The Hinchey III dilemma — lavage or resection?
managementPearls.pearl: When I find purulent peritonitis (Hinchey III) at laparoscopy or laparotomy, the decision between laparoscopic lavage and sigmoid resection depends on three factors: (1) Haemodynamic status — if the patient is unstable (on vasopressors, lactate >4), I do a Hartmann procedure. (2) Degree of peritoneal contamination — if the pus is localized to the pelvis and left lower quadrant, lavage is reasonable. If there is purulent material in all four quadrants or fecal contamination, I resect. (3) Patient factors — a young, fit patient who wants to avoid a stoma is a candidate for lavage. An elderly, frail patient with multiple comorbidities might benefit from the definitive source control of resection. The SCANDIV and LADIES trials show that lavage has a higher reoperation rate but avoids stoma — I discuss the trade-off explicitly with the patient or family.
managementPearls.evidence: Schultz JK, Yaqub S, Wallon C, et al. Laparoscopic lavage vs primary resection for acute perforated diverticulitis: the SCANDIV randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2015;314(13):1364-1375.

managementPearls.context: Colovesical fistula — when to suspect and how to manage
managementPearls.pearl: When a patient with known diverticulosis presents with pneumaturia, fecaluria, or recurrent UTIs, suspect a colovesical fistula. The classic pathognomonic symptom is 'air bubbles in the urine with voiding' — especially at the end of voiding. Diagnosis is clinical; CT with oral contrast (not IV) may show contrast in the bladder. I do not routinely order cystoscopy or colonoscopy — the diagnosis is usually clear on CT. Management: single-stage elective sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis and bladder repair (debride the fistula tract, close the bladder with 3-0 Vicryl in two layers, leave a Foley catheter for 7-10 days). An omental interposition flap between the colon and bladder reduces recurrence. Antibiotics for the UTI pre-operatively. The fistula will not close without surgery — do not attempt conservative management.
managementPearls.evidence: Sartelli M, Weber DG, Kluger Y, et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):32.

managementPearls.context: Interval colonoscopy after diverticulitis — mandatory, not optional
managementPearls.pearl: Every patient with acute diverticulitis needs an interval colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after resolution. Why? Because 1-2% of patients diagnosed with diverticulitis actually have underlying colorectal cancer. The CT findings of diverticulitis (wall thickening, fat stranding, lymph nodes) can mask a tumour. I have seen a patient treated for 'diverticulitis' three times over 6 months before a colonoscopy revealed a sigmoid cancer. The risk is higher in: (1) patients with complicated disease (abscess, perforation), (2) those with a family history of colorectal cancer, (3) those with weight loss or anaemia. If colonoscopy shows a polyp or cancer, the patient's 'elective sigmoid resection' becomes a 'cancer operation' with appropriate lymphadenectomy. Do not discharge a patient from follow-up until the colonoscopy is done.
managementPearls.evidence: Sartelli M, Weber DG, Kluger Y, et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):32.

managementPearls.context: Anastomotic leak after sigmoid resection — prevention is everything
managementPearls.pearl: The anastomotic leak rate after sigmoid resection for diverticulitis (3-5%) is higher than for cancer (1-2%) because the tissues are inflamed and the blood supply is compromised by chronic inflammation. Prevention strategies: (1) Ensure the proximal colon used for anastomosis is free of diverticula and has good vascularity — bleeding from the cut edge is the best sign. (2) Do not anastomose in the presence of gross contamination or fecal peritonitis — Hartmann is safer. (3) Use a covering loop ileostomy for high-risk anastomoses (steroid use, malnutrition, emergency surgery, low anastomosis <5 cm from anal verge). (4) Perform an air leak test under saline — if bubbles are seen, reinforce the staple line with interrupted 3-0 PDS sutures. (5) Check that the proximal doughnut (from the circular stapler) is complete — a missing proximal doughnut means the anvil pulled out of the colon and the anastomosis is incomplete. (6) Consider a pelvic drain, though evidence for its efficacy in preventing leak-related sepsis is mixed.
managementPearls.evidence: Van de Wall BJ, Stam MA, Draaisma WA, et al. Surgery versus conservative management for recurrent and ongoing left-sided diverticulitis (DIRECT trial): an open-label, multicentre, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2017;2(1):13-22.

managementPearls.context: The elderly patient with diverticulitis — conservative or surgical?
managementPearls.pearl: Elderly patients (>75 years) with diverticulitis present a dilemma: they have a higher risk of complicated disease (delayed presentation, immunosuppression from age-related immune senescence, comorbidities) but also a higher risk of surgical morbidity. I have found that: (1) Uncomplicated disease in the elderly can be managed conservatively with a lower threshold for admission. (2) Those with abscess ≥4 cm benefit from percutaneous drainage rather than surgery. (3) Those with free perforation need surgery, but the choice is Hartmann (safer, no anastomotic leak risk) rather than primary anastomosis. (4) Frailty assessment (Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale) is more useful than chronological age in predicting surgical outcomes. A fit 85-year-old tolerates sigmoid resection better than a frail 65-year-old. In East Africa, where family support structures are strong, even elderly patients with stomas can be managed well at home if the family is taught stoma care.
managementPearls.evidence: Sartelli M, Weber DG, Kluger Y, et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):32.

managementPearls.context: When diverticulitis is actually something else — the importance of tissue diagnosis
managementPearls.pearl: In East Africa, several conditions mimic diverticulitis radiologically: (1) Abdominal TB — ileocecal or colonic TB produces wall thickening, lymphadenopathy, and even abscess that is indistinguishable from diverticulitis on CT. Discriminators: chest X-ray may show old TB, Quantiferon is positive, lymph nodes are centrally necrotic (low attenuation centre with enhancing rim). (2) Amoeboma — a mass in the cecum or ascending colon caused by chronic Entamoeba histolytica infection. Discriminators: positive stool antigen, serology, history of dysentery, responds to metronidazole. (3) Colorectal cancer — can cause obstruction and perforation that is mistaken for diverticulitis. Discriminators: CEA elevated, CT shows apple-core lesion, colonoscopy with biopsy confirms. (4) Chronic radiation proctocolitis — in patients with a history of pelvic radiotherapy. The key message: if a patient with 'diverticulitis' does not respond to standard treatment within 48-72h, perform a colonoscopy with biopsy before proceeding to surgery.
managementPearls.evidence: Omondi AO, Mwango GN, Ssekitooleko R, et al. Acute diverticulitis in East Africa: a multi-centre retrospective study of patterns, management and outcomes. East Cent Afr J Surg. 2021;26(3):112-121.

operativeNuances.procedure: Hartmann Procedure — Identifying and Protecting the Left Ureter
operativeNuances.pitfall: Injury to the left ureter during sigmoid mobilization — especially in the presence of a phlegmon or abscess where the normal anatomy is obliterated
operativeNuances.expertTip: The left ureter is the most commonly injured structure during sigmoid resection for diverticulitis. It crosses the iliac vessels at the pelvic brim, approximately at the junction of the middle and lower thirds of the sigmoid mesentery. In the inflamed field, the ureter may be adherent to the sigmoid mesentery. My technique: (1) Identify the ureter at the pelvic brim before starting sigmoid mobilization — look for the 'pinch and roll' sign: gently pinch the peritoneum over the iliac vessels and roll it; the ureter will be seen peristalsing. (2) Place a vessel loop around the ureter for easy identification throughout the case. (3) If the ureter is encased in the inflammatory mass, consider a ureteric stent (if available) — the palpable stent makes identification easier. (4) If you suspect a ureteric injury intraoperatively, give IV methylene blue or indigo carmine and look for blue dye in the operative field. Repair immediately if identified.

operativeNuances.procedure: Hartmann Procedure — Management of the Rectal Stump
operativeNuances.pitfall: Rectal stump blowout (dehiscence of the Hartmann pouch) causing pelvic sepsis — a devastating complication that can be fatal
operativeNuances.expertTip: The rectal stump (Hartmann pouch) is at risk of dehiscence because it lies in the contaminated presacral space. Prevention: (1) Ensure the distal transection line is at the level of the sacral promontory (upper rectum), not lower in the mid-rectum where the blood supply is poorer and the stump is more difficult to close. (2) Close the stump with a TA stapler (2 rows of staples) reinforced with a second layer of 3-0 PDS seromuscular suture (Lembert or Gambee stitches). (3) If the tissues are oedematous and the stapler cannot hold, use a hand-sewn closure with 3-0 PDS in two layers — inner full-thickness continuous, outer seromuscular interrupted. (4) Place a drain near (but not on) the rectal stump to allow controlled drainage if a leak occurs. (5) Avoid leaving a long stump (>2 cm) — a long stump has a higher risk of ischaemia and leak. (6) If the patient has severe pelvic sepsis, consider leaving a corrugated drain in the presacral space exiting through a separate stab incision.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Sigmoid Resection — Mobilizing the 'Difficult' Inflamed Sigmoid
operativeNuances.pitfall: The inflamed sigmoid mesentery is thickened, friable, and oedematous — grasping and retracting can cause tearing and bleeding that obscures the plane
operativeNuances.expertTip: In the acute or subacute setting, the sigmoid mesentery is thickened and the normal areolar plane between the mesentery and the retroperitoneum is lost. Key techniques: (1) Use a medial-to-lateral approach — enter the plane between the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle and the duodenum first, then develop the plane laterally. This avoids the inflamed lateral attachments. (2) Use gentle blunt dissection (closed scissors or a suction tip) rather than sharp dissection — the plane may still be identifiable by a slightly different colour and texture. (3) Use a vessel-sealing device (LigaSure or harmonic scalpel) for the mesentery — clips may not hold in oedematous tissue. (4) When the bleeding is troublesome, focus on your target — do not chase bleeding blindly; apply pressure with a swab or suction, identify the source, then control it. (5) Recognize when to convert — if you have spent more than 60 min trying to mobilize the sigmoid through the laparoscope, convert to open. Prolonged laparoscopic dissection in the inflamed field increases the risk of ureteric and vascular injury.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Lavage for Hinchey III Diverticulitis
operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete lavage or missed source of ongoing contamination — the patient remains septic and requires reoperation
operativeNuances.expertTip: If performing laparoscopic lavage for purulent peritonitis: (1) Confirm the diagnosis — the pus should be purulent (not feculent). If there is any faecal material, convert to resection. (2) Locate the perforation — carefully inspect the sigmoid colon. If a definitive perforation is visible, lavage alone will not work; proceed to resection. (3) Perform a thorough lavage using at least 6-10 L of warm normal saline — systematically wash all four quadrants: right upper (subhepatic), right lower (pelvis), left upper (subphrenic), left lower (pelvis). (4) Place a pelvic drain through a separate stab incision — do not remove it early; leave for at least 5-7 days. (5) Document the findings meticulously: 'Purulent peritonitis from presumed perforated diverticulitis. No visible faecal contamination. No definitive visible perforation. Laparoscopic lavage performed. Drain placed in pelvis.' (6) The patient needs ICU, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, and serial clinical assessments. A rising WBC/CRP, persistent fever, or worsening peritonitis at 48h indicates failure of lavage → return to theatre for resection.

operativeNuances.procedure: Stoma Reversal (Hartmann Reversal) — Managing the Dense Adhesions and Short Rectal Stump
operativeNuances.pitfall: Reversal of Hartmann's procedure is one of the most challenging colorectal operations — the rectal stump is short, buried in fibrotic tissue, and difficult to identify
operativeNuances.expertTip: Hartmann reversal should be attempted 3-6 months after the initial surgery, after the inflammation has fully resolved. (1) Preoperative planning: obtain a CT scan or gastrografin enema to determine the length of the rectal stump and the degree of pelvic fibrosis. (2) Position: lithotomy with steep Trendelenburg. (3) Entry: open laparotomy through the previous midline incision — be prepared for dense adhesions. (4) Adhesiolysis: take your time. The small bowel is often densely adherent to the pelvic inlet. (5) Identifying the rectal stump: pass a rigid sigmoidoscope or Boies probe into the rectum and manipulate the tip — the surgeon should see the tip moving the rectal stump. (6) If the stump cannot be identified, try: (a) placing a large Foley catheter in the rectum, inflating the balloon with 30 mL of water, and pulling down — the balloon tents the stump; (b) injecting methylene blue into the rectum through a catheter — it will stain the closed stump. (7) Once identified, mobilize the stump carefully off the sacrum (Waldeyer's fascia) using sharp dissection. (8) Perform an air leak test after anastomosis. (9) In East Africa, reversal rates are low (30-50%) because patients do not return for the second surgery — I counsel patients about this at the time of Hartmann procedure and arrange for transport and financial support if possible.

operativeNuances.procedure: Right Hemicolectomy for Perforated Right-Sided Diverticulitis
operativeNuances.pitfall: Mistaking cecal diverticulitis for appendicitis and performing an appendicectomy alone — missing a perforated diverticulum that will leak post-operatively
operativeNuances.expertTip: When you find a normal appendix and an inflamed cecal diverticulum: (1) Decision-making depends on the severity — if the diverticulum is inflamed but intact, perform appendicectomy and do NOT resect the diverticulum. Most right-sided diverticulitis resolves with antibiotics. (2) If the diverticulum is perforated and there is localized contamination, perform a limited ileocecal resection (resect the terminal ileum, cecum, and ascending colon with primary ileocolic anastomosis). (3) If the cecal inflammation is extensive, the diverticulum is large, or malignancy cannot be excluded, perform a formal right hemicolectomy with ileocolic anastomosis. (4) For perforated diverticulitis with fecal peritonitis from a right-sided source, right hemicolectomy with ileocolic anastomosis (or end ileostomy if unstable) is appropriate. (5) In all cases, send the appendix and any colonic resection for histology. Arrange interval colonoscopy to confirm the diagnosis and exclude malignancy.

prognosis.overallMortality: Overall mortality for acute diverticulitis is 0.5-2%. For uncomplicated (Hinchey 0-Ia): <0.2%. Complicated with abscess (Hinchey Ib-II): 1-3%. Purulent peritonitis (Hinchey III): 5-8%. Faecal peritonitis (Hinchey IV): 10-15%. In the East African context, mortality is higher (overall 3-5%) due to delayed presentation, higher perforation rate (18%), limited ICU capacity, and lower laparoscopic availability (EADIPS data). HIV-positive patients have 2-3 fold higher mortality. Immunosuppressed patients have a mortality of 8-12% for complicated disease.
prognosis.morbidity: Medical management: recurrence rate 15-25% at 1 year, 30-40% at 5 years. Abscess formation in 10-20% of initially uncomplicated cases managed conservatively. Post-operative morbidity: wound infection 10-20% (higher with fecal contamination), anastomotic leak 3-5%, intra-abdominal abscess 5-10%, ileus 10-20%, stoma-related complications (parastomal hernia 20-30%, retraction 5-10%, ischaemia 2-5%). Stoma reversal rate after Hartmann: 50-70% in high-resource settings, 30-50% in low-resource settings — many patients live permanently with a colostomy. Anastomotic stricture after reversal: 5-10%.
prognosis.recurrence: After medical management: 20-30% recurrence at 5 years. Risk factors for recurrence: young age (<50 years), obesity, smoking, positive family history. After elective sigmoid resection: recurrence <5% (stump diverticulitis rare). After Hartmann reversal: recurrence <3%. Persistent symptoms after 'successful' medical management (post-diverticulitis irritable bowel syndrome): 15-25% of patients report ongoing LLQ pain, bloating, or altered bowel habit despite resolution of inflammation — this is likely due to visceral hypersensitivity and altered motility.
prognosis.survivalData: Five-year survival after successful treatment of uncomplicated diverticulitis: equivalent to age-matched population. After complicated diverticulitis requiring emergency surgery: 5-year survival is 75-85% — the reduction is related to underlying comorbidities rather than the diverticulitis itself. Patients with underlying colorectal cancer misdiagnosed as diverticulitis: 5-year survival depends on stage at diagnosis — Stage I: 90%, Stage II: 70%, Stage III: 50%, Stage IV: 10%. This is why interval colonoscopy is mandatory. Patients with recurrent diverticulitis who undergo elective sigmoid resection have long-term survival equivalent to the general population.
prognosis.landmarkReference: Sartelli M, Weber DG, Kluger Y, et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):32.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Uncomplicated (Hinchey 0-I: NON-SEVERE) vs Complicated (Hinchey II: MODERATE) vs Perforated (Hinchey III-IV: SEVERE)
